title: Dyeing with fennel
subtitle: Bright yellow from fennel fronds and stems on alum-mordanted wool
excerpt: Garden fennel (Foeniculum vulgare) produces a clear, warm yellow on alum-mordanted wool from its foliage and stems. The dye is extracted by simmering, and the yield is good from a modest amount of plant material. Use the fronds cut in summer when colour is strongest.
sourceNotes: Fennel as a natural dye documented in Jenny Dean, Wild Colour (1999, in-copyright, substance only referenced), and in Pellew, Dyes and Dyeing (1913, public domain). Fennel contains quercetin and other flavonoid dye compounds that produce a reliable yellow on alum-mordanted protein fibres.

glossaryTerms.definition: A metallic salt that bonds between the dye and the fibre to fix the colour. Without mordant, fennel colour washes out within a few washes.
The pot of extracted fennel dye liquid in which the mordanted fibre is placed to absorb colour.

A class of plant dye compounds (including quercetin, luteolin, and kaempferol) that produce yellows and oranges on alum-mordanted fibres. Fennel and many other yellow dye plants get their colour from flavonoids.

content.text: that yields a clear yellow from its
compounds. The colour takes well on alum
ed wool and holds reasonably well for an indoor-use textile.

attrs.title: Mordant safety
attrs.body: Dye-pots and utensils are dedicated. Wear gloves and apron. Work outdoors or with ventilation. Alum is low-hazard; keep away from food.

items.name: Fresh fennel fronds and stems (cut in summer)

content.text: Dissolve alum in hot water and add to the dye-pot with cold water to cover the fibre. Add the pre-wetted wool and raise to 80 degrees Celsius over 30 minutes. Hold for 45 minutes and allow to cool before removing.

Chop the fennel fronds and stems and place in the dye-pot with enough water to cover. Bring to a simmer and hold at 80 to 90 degrees Celsius for 45 minutes. Strain through a cloth into a clean pot, discarding the plant material. The dye bath will be a warm golden yellow.
Add the mordanted, damp fibre to the dye bath. Raise slowly to 80 degrees Celsius and hold for 45 minutes, stirring gently every 10 minutes. Remove and rinse in progressively cooler water.
The alum mordant bath and the fennel dye bath can be composted or poured down the foul drain in small home quantities. No iron or copper mordants are used in this recipe.

An iron after-bath shifts the yellow toward a warm olive-green. Fennel seeds simmer longer and produce a slightly deeper, more golden colour than the foliage alone; combine seeds and fronds for the strongest dye bath.

items.problem: The colour looks strongly yellow in the pot but rinses out to a pale straw colour
items.cause: The mordant was not fully incorporated into the fibre before dyeing, or the fibre was not pre-wetted
items.fix: Ensure the fibre is fully saturated before entering both the mordant bath and the dye bath. A dry fibre repels liquid at the fibre surface and takes an uneven mordant.
